=== TRADITIONS ===: Array of 14 new religious traditions for Central/North Asia, Southeast Asia, and South Asia

traditions.name: Tengrism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE or earlier (prehistoric steppe origins, attested by Xiongnu period c. 3rd century BCE)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Central Asian steppe (modern Mongolia, southern Siberia, Kazakhstan)

keyFigures.name: Tengri
keyFigures.role: Supreme sky god, Eternal Blue Sky, ruler of all existence

keyFigures.name: Umay
keyFigures.role: Mother goddess of fertility, virginity, and children

keyFigures.name: Erlik
keyFigures.role: God of death and the underworld, ruler of the Lower World

keyFigures.name: Genghis Khan
keyFigures.role: Proclaimed chosen of Tengri; made Tengrism state religion of the Mongol Empire

keyFigures.name: Bilge Khagan
keyFigures.role: Gokturk ruler who proclaimed Tengri as supreme deity in Orkhon inscriptions (8th century CE)

keyConcepts: Tengri (Eternal Blue Sky as supreme deity)
Three-layered cosmos (Upper World, Middle World, Lower World)
Kut (divine mandate/grace from Tengri to rulers)
Harmony with nature as life's purpose
Ancestor worship and veneration
Kumys (fermented mare's milk) ritual offerings
Religious tolerance rooted in universal spiritual power
Shamanic mediation between cosmic realms
traditions.evidence: Orkhon inscriptions (8th c. CE), Herodotus accounts of Scythian sky worship, Secret History of the Mongols (13th c.), archaeological kurgans across Central Asian steppe. Modern revival movements in Tuva, Buryatia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan.

traditions.name: Bon (Yungdrung Bon)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1800 BCE (traditional dating; Zhang Zhung kingdom origins, pre-Buddhist Tibet)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Zhang Zhung kingdom (western Tibet), Olmo Lungring

keyFigures.name: Tonpa Shenrab Miwoche
keyFigures.role: Founder and supreme teacher (Buddha of Bon), born in Olmo Lungring

keyFigures.name: Shenlha Okar
keyFigures.role: Transcendent deity of white light who transmitted teachings to Tonpa Shenrab

keyFigures.name: Sidpa Gyalmo
keyFigures.role: Queen of the World, chief protectress of Bon tradition

keyFigures.name: Drenpa Namkha
keyFigures.role: 8th-century Bon master who preserved teachings during Buddhist persecution

keyConcepts: Yungdrung (eternal/indestructible swastika symbol, counterclockwise)
Five elements (earth, water, fire, air, space)
Karma and cyclic rebirth (samsara)
Zhang Zhung (ancient kingdom and sacred geography)
Dzogchen (Great Perfection meditation, shared with Nyingma Buddhism)
Three jewels: body, speech, and mind
Olmo Lungring (sacred semi-mythical homeland)
Soul retrieval and exorcism rituals
traditions.evidence: Zhang Zhung manuscripts, Bon canonical texts (Kanjur and Tenjur), Dunhuang documents, archaeological sites in western Tibet. Menri Monastery in Dolanji (India) serves as the tradition's main exile center. Recognized as fifth spiritual tradition of Tibet by Dalai Lama.

traditions.name: Siberian Shamanism
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 8000 BCE or earlier (prehistoric hunter-gatherer origins across Northern Eurasia)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Siberia (Evenki, Yakut/Sakha, Buryat, Tuvan, Khanty, Nenets territories)

keyFigures.name: Ulgen/Ulgan
keyFigures.role: Creator sky deity of the Upper World (Altai and Buryat traditions)

keyFigures.name: Erlik Khan
keyFigures.role: Lord of the dead and ruler of the Lower World

keyFigures.name: Abaasy
keyFigures.role: Spirits of the Yakut Lower World, source of disease and misfortune

keyFigures.name: Ai-Toyon
keyFigures.role: Yakut supreme deity, lord of light who created the first shaman

keyConcepts: World Tree (axis mundi connecting three cosmic realms)
Three-world cosmology (Upper, Middle, Lower worlds)
Shamanic ecstatic trance via drumming and chanting
Spirit journey (soul flight to other realms)
Drum as shamanic horse or boat for spirit travel
Animal spirit helpers and totemic ancestry
Reindeer and bear cult veneration
Soul retrieval and healing ceremonies
traditions.evidence: Ethnographic records by Shirokogoroff (1935) on Evenki shamanism, Yakut olonkho epics (UNESCO Intangible Heritage), Buryat ritual texts, Pazyryk frozen tombs with shamanic artifacts (5th-3rd c. BCE), rock art across Siberia dating to Neolithic period.

traditions.name: Mongolian Shamanism (Boo Morgol)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 400-300 BCE (earliest attested Mongolic shamanic practices)

keyFigures.name: Qormusta Tengri
keyFigures.role: Chief deity of the 99 Tengers, supreme heavenly lord

keyFigures.name: Zayagachi
keyFigures.role: Goddess of fate who determines each person's destiny

keyFigures.name: Dayan Deerh
keyFigures.role: Powerful ancestral shaman spirit and legendary warrior

keyFigures.name: Genghis Khan
keyFigures.role: Elevated to divine ancestor spirit (sulde) after death, national shamanic figure

keyConcepts: 99 Tengers (heavenly deities: 55 western benevolent, 44 eastern malevolent)
Ongon (spirit helpers: ancestral, animal, and nature spirits)
Ovoo (sacred stone cairns as spirit dwelling places)
Hengereg (shamanic drum as vehicle for spirit travel)
Sulde (soul/spirit force, especially of warriors and khans)
Libation offerings of airag (fermented mare's milk)
Secret History of the Mongols cosmological framework
Eternal Blue Sky and Mother Earth duality
traditions.evidence: Secret History of the Mongols (1228 CE), archaeological ovoo sites, Mongolian shamanic drum collections (National Museum of Mongolia), ethnographic fieldwork by Heissig (1980) and Pegg (2001). Post-Soviet revival since 1990s with thousands of practicing shamans.

traditions.name: Scythian Religion
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 900-700 BCE (emergence of Scythian culture in Pontic steppe)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Pontic-Caspian steppe (modern Ukraine, southern Russia, Kazakhstan)

keyFigures.name: Tabiti
keyFigures.role: Supreme goddess of fire and the hearth, most venerated deity (equated with Greek Hestia)

keyFigures.name: Papaeus
keyFigures.role: Sky father god, personification of Heaven (equated with Zeus)

keyFigures.name: Api
keyFigures.role: Earth mother goddess, consort of Papaeus

keyFigures.name: Goetosyrus/Oetosyrus
keyFigures.role: Sun god (equated with Apollo)

keyFigures.name: Argimpasa/Artimpasa
keyFigures.role: Goddess of love and fertility (equated with Aphrodite Urania)

keyFigures.name: Thagimasadas
keyFigures.role: Sea/water god, worshipped by Royal Scythians (equated with Poseidon)

keyConcepts: Seven-deity pantheon (as recorded by Herodotus)
Aniconic worship (no statues except for Ares/war-god sword altar)
Hemp-smoke purification and ecstatic rituals
Animal-style art as religious symbolism
Sword-altar cult for war-god worship
Royal funerary rites with human and horse sacrifice
Oath-swearing by the royal hearth (Tabiti)
Enarei (androgynous shamanic seers blessed by Argimpasa)
traditions.evidence: Herodotus Histories Book IV (c. 440 BCE), Pazyryk frozen tombs with hemp-burning apparatus, gold pectoral from Tovsta Mohyla, Tillia Tepe treasure (Afghanistan), Chertomlyk vase depicting rituals. Iranian linguistic substrate confirms Indo-Iranian religious heritage.

traditions.name: Tai Folk Religion (Satsana Phi)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE or earlier (prehistoric Tai animist beliefs, pre-Buddhist period)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Southeast Asian mainland (present-day Thailand, Laos, Shan State of Myanmar)

keyFigures.name: Phi Fa
keyFigures.role: Sky spirit, supreme celestial deity

keyFigures.name: Phi Muang
keyFigures.role: Guardian spirit of the city/kingdom

keyFigures.name: Phi Ban
keyFigures.role: Village guardian spirits who protect communities

keyFigures.name: Nang Kwak
keyFigures.role: Spirit of prosperity and good fortune, popular household deity

keyFigures.name: Phi Pha (or Thaen)
keyFigures.role: Heavenly deities of the Lao-Isan tradition

keyConcepts: Phi (spirits inhabiting natural features, ancestors, places)
Khwan (32 body spirits/souls that can wander from the body)
Baci/Su Khwan ceremony (ritual to recall wandering khwan)
Spirit houses (san phra phum) for guardian spirits
Maw phi (spirit doctors/mediums who communicate with phi)
Ancestral veneration and offerings at household shrines
Syncretism with Theravada Buddhism and Hindu Brahmanism
Taboos related to angering local phi spirits
traditions.evidence: Ban Chiang archaeological site (3600 BCE) with spirit-related burial practices, Lao Isan ethnographic records, Thai spirit house tradition documented since Sukhothai period (13th c. CE). Baci ceremony remains widespread across Laos and northeast Thailand.

traditions.name: Balinese Hinduism (Agama Hindu Dharma)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1st century CE (Indian Hindu-Buddhist influence arrives in Indonesian archipelago; Bali's unique form solidified by 11th century)

keyFigures.name: Sang Hyang Widhi Wasa
keyFigures.role: Supreme God, the one all-pervading divine being (Acintya)

keyFigures.name: Dang Hyang Nirartha
keyFigures.role: 16th-century Javanese Brahmin priest who reformed Balinese Hinduism

keyFigures.name: Mpu Kuturan
keyFigures.role: 11th-century sage who established the tri murti temple system in Bali

keyFigures.name: Ida Pedanda
keyFigures.role: High priest (Brahmin) lineage that maintains Vedic liturgy in Bali

keyConcepts: Tri Hita Karana (three causes of well-being: harmony with divine, humans, nature)
Canang sari (daily flower and incense offerings to spirits and gods)
Kaja-kelod (sacred directional orientation: mountain = holy, sea = impure)
Nyepi (Day of Silence, Balinese New Year)
Subak (sacred irrigation system, UNESCO World Heritage)
Sekala and Niskala (visible and invisible worlds)
Yadnya (five categories of ritual sacrifice)
Tri Murti (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva as cosmic functions)
traditions.evidence: Prasasti (copper inscriptions) dating from 9th century CE in Bali, Besakih temple complex (at least 13th century), Majapahit-era literary texts (Kakawin), UNESCO inscription of subak system (2012) as embodiment of Tri Hita Karana philosophy.

traditions.name: Vietnamese Folk Religion (Dao Mau / Tu Phu)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE or earlier (ancient Lac Viet animist origins; Dao Mau formalized by 16th century CE)

keyFigures.name: Lieu Hanh (Mau Lieu Hanh)
keyFigures.role: Mother Goddess of Earth, one of Vietnam's Four Immortals, chief deity of Dao Mau

keyFigures.name: Mau Thuong Thien
keyFigures.role: Mother Goddess of Heaven, ruler of the Heaven Palace (Thien Phu)

keyFigures.name: Mau Thoai
keyFigures.role: Mother Goddess of Water, ruler of the Water Palace

keyFigures.name: Mau Thuong Ngan
keyFigures.role: Mother Goddess of Mountains and Forests

keyFigures.name: Tran Hung Dao
keyFigures.role: 13th-century Vietnamese national hero, deified as powerful spirit in the Four Palaces pantheon

keyConcepts: Tu Phu (Four Palaces: Heaven, Earth, Water, Mountains/Forests)
Hau Dong/Len Dong (spirit mediumship ritual with trance possession)
Mau (Mother Goddess worship as central devotion)
Color symbolism (red=heaven, yellow=earth, white=water, green=mountains)
Ancestor veneration and household altars
Deification of historical heroes as spirits
Tam Phu (Three Palaces, earlier form of Four Palaces)
UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage (2016)
traditions.evidence: UNESCO inscription of practices related to Mother Goddess worship (2016), temple complexes in Northern Vietnam (Phu Day temple), 16th-century literary references to Lieu Hanh, ethnographic studies by Ngo Duc Thinh and others.

traditions.name: Philippine Indigenous Religion (Anitism)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE or earlier (Austronesian animist traditions predating colonial contact)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Philippine archipelago (Tagalog, Visayan, Cordilleran, Lumad, and other regions)

keyFigures.name: Bathala
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator god (Maykapal/Lumikha) of the Tagalog people

keyFigures.name: Laon
keyFigures.role: Supreme creator deity of the Visayan peoples, the Ancient One

keyFigures.name: Kabunyan
keyFigures.role: Creator and supreme teacher of the Cordilleran peoples (Igorot)

keyFigures.name: Babaylan
keyFigures.role: Predominantly female shamanic priestesses, spiritual leaders and healers

keyFigures.name: Manama
keyFigures.role: Highest god of the Manuvu (Bagobo) peoples of Mindanao

keyConcepts: Anito (ancestor spirits and general spirit entities)
Diwata (nature spirits and deities inhabiting trees, rivers, mountains)
Babaylan (female shamanic priestesses as spiritual intermediaries)
Pag-anito (seance ritual for communicating with ancestor spirits)
Dambana/Latangan (temporary bamboo altars for offerings)
Asog/Bayok (gender-crossing male spiritual practitioners)
Bathala and regional supreme creator deities
Sacred groves and natural sites as spirit dwellings
traditions.evidence: Spanish colonial accounts (Boxer Codex 1590, Plasencia's Customs 1589), pre-colonial gold artifacts with deity imagery (Agusan gold image), Ifugao hudhud chants (UNESCO), living Lumad and Cordilleran traditions. Archaeological evidence from Tabon Cave (c. 47,000 BP) and Manunggul Jar (890-710 BCE).

traditions.name: Kejawen (Javanese Mysticism)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 500 BCE (ancient Javanese animist roots; syncretic form developed through Hindu-Buddhist period 5th-15th c. CE and Islamic period from 15th c.)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Java, Indonesia (especially Central and East Java)

keyFigures.name: Dewi Sri
keyFigures.role: Rice goddess of fertility and prosperity, central to agricultural ritual

keyFigures.name: Sunan Kalijaga
keyFigures.role: One of the Wali Songo (Nine Saints) who synthesized Islam with Javanese mysticism

keyFigures.name: Sultan Agung
keyFigures.role: 17th-century Mataram sultan who unified Islamic and Javanese mystical calendars

keyFigures.name: Ratu Kidul
keyFigures.role: Queen of the South Sea (Indian Ocean), powerful spirit consort of Javanese kings

keyConcepts: Kebatinan (inner spiritual science, pursuit of inner harmony)
Batin (inner self/spiritual purity as highest value)
Rasa (inner feeling/intuition as path to divine union)
Slametan (communal ritual feast for spiritual harmony and life transitions)
Semedi (meditation and ascetic practice for spiritual power)
Wahyu (divine revelation/spiritual mandate for rulers)
Abangan (nominal Muslims practicing Javanese mysticism)
Harmony with ancestral spirits and nature spirits
traditions.evidence: Serat Centhini (19th-century Javanese encyclopedia of mysticism), Babad Tanah Jawi (chronicle of Javanese kingdoms), Clifford Geertz's Religion of Java (1960) ethnographic classification (abangan/santri/priyayi), living slametan tradition in rural Java.

traditions.name: Caodaism (Dao Cao Dai)
traditions.originDateLabel: 1926 CE (officially established in Tay Ninh, southern Vietnam)

keyFigures.name: Ngo Van Chieu
keyFigures.role: Founding medium, colonial official who first received Cao Dai revelations in seances (1920s)

keyFigures.name: Le Van Trung
keyFigures.role: First Giao Tong (Pope) of Caodaism

keyFigures.name: Pham Cong Tac
keyFigures.role: Ho Phap (spiritual protector/medium), key organizer of the Cao Dai church

keyFigures.name: Victor Hugo
keyFigures.role: Deified as a spirit saint; serves as Chief Spirit of Foreign Missions

keyFigures.name: Ly Thai Bach (Li Bai)
keyFigures.role: Deified Tang dynasty poet, serves as head spirit of Caodai spiritual hierarchy

keyConcepts: Cao Dai (Supreme Being, ultimate creator, 'High Tower')
Divine Eye (All-Seeing Eye symbol representing God's omniscience)
Third Amnesty (belief that Caodaism is God's third and final revelation to humanity)
Universal religion (all faiths share a common divine origin)
Spiritism/seance communication with divine spirits
Vegetarianism and moral precepts
Hierarchical priesthood modeled on Catholic structure
Synthesis of Five Branches: Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism, Christianity, Islam
traditions.evidence: Tay Ninh Holy See (constructed 1933-1955), approximately 4-6 million adherents in Vietnam (government census), Canon Codifier texts from seance sessions, recognition by Vietnamese government. Holy See is a major tourist site.

traditions.name: Muism (Korean Shamanism / Sinism)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 3000 BCE or earlier (prehistoric Korean peninsular origins, Dangun mythology dated to 2333 BCE)

keyFigures.name: Dangun
keyFigures.role: Legendary divine founder of Gojoseon (2333 BCE), son of Hwanung and bear-woman, later became Mountain God

keyFigures.name: Hwanin
keyFigures.role: Supreme celestial deity, lord of heaven, grandfather of Dangun

keyFigures.name: Hwanung
keyFigures.role: Divine prince who descended from heaven to establish sacred civilization on earth

keyFigures.name: Sanshin
keyFigures.role: Mountain God, protector of Korean land, depicted as white-bearded elder with tiger

keyFigures.name: Princess Bari
keyFigures.role: Mythological abandoned princess who journeyed to the underworld; patron saint of mudang shamans

keyConcepts: Mudang (shamanic practitioners, predominantly female, who perform gut rituals)
Gut/Kut (elaborate shamanic rituals with drumming, dance, costume changes, and spirit possession)
Sanshin (Mountain God worship at dedicated shrines on every major mountain)
Dangun mythology (divine origin of Korean nation from heavenly descent)
Sesseumu and Kangshinmu (two types of mudang: hereditary vs. spirit-called)
Samsin Halmoni (grandmother goddess of childbirth and fate)
Jeoseung Saja (psychopomp spirit who guides dead to afterlife)
Han (deep collective sorrow/unrequited grief, resolved through shamanic ritual)
traditions.evidence: Samguk Yusa (1281 CE) records Dangun myth, Yi dynasty-era mudang paintings, modern gut rituals recognized as Korean Intangible Cultural Heritage, active shrines to Sanshin throughout South Korea. Estimated 40,000-50,000 practicing mudang in contemporary South Korea.

traditions.name: Ayyavazhi
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1833 CE (incarnation of Ayya Vaikundar in Swamithope, Tamil Nadu)
traditions.geographicOrigin: Kanyakumari and Tirunelveli districts, Tamil Nadu, South India

keyFigures.name: Ayya Vaikundar
keyFigures.role: Purna avatar (full incarnation) of Narayana, founder and central figure

keyFigures.name: Narayana
keyFigures.role: Supreme God who sends Vaikundar as incarnation to destroy evil age

keyFigures.name: Kroni/Kaliyan
keyFigures.role: Primordial evil being, source of all suffering, antagonist destroyed by Vaikundar

keyFigures.name: Hari Gopalan
keyFigures.role: First disciple and key propagator of Ayyavazhi teachings

keyConcepts: Akilam (Akilathirattu Ammanai: primary scripture narrating cosmic history and Vaikundar's mission)
Arul Nool (secondary scripture with prayers, hymns, and rituals)
Ekam (divine oneness/unity of God)
Dharma Yukam (age of righteousness that Vaikundar inaugurates)
Vinchai (evil force of Kali age that Vaikundar destroys)
Thuvayal Thavisu (sacred throne worship without idol images)
Thirunamam (flame-shaped symbol atop lotus, ideological emblem)
Social equality and rejection of caste discrimination
traditions.evidence: Akilathirattu Ammanai manuscript (dictated 1841), Swamithope pathi (headquarters temple), network of over 8,000 worship centers across Tamil Nadu, declared state holiday in Kanyakumari district (1994). Estimated several million adherents in southern Tamil Nadu.

traditions.name: Lingayatism (Basava Movement)
traditions.originDateLabel: c. 1150 CE (Basavanna's reform movement in Kalyana, Karnataka)

keyFigures.name: Basavanna (Basaveshwara)
keyFigures.role: Founder, 12th-century philosopher, poet, prime minister under King Bijjala II

keyFigures.name: Akka Mahadevi
keyFigures.role: Prominent female mystic poet, radical renunciant and spiritual teacher

keyFigures.name: Allama Prabhu
keyFigures.role: Chief mystic and president of the Anubhava Mantapa spiritual assembly

keyFigures.name: Channabasavanna
keyFigures.role: Basavanna's nephew, systematizer of Lingayat theology and practice

keyFigures.name: Siddarama
keyFigures.role: Prolific vachana poet and humanitarian who emphasized service to the poor

keyConcepts: Ishtalinga (personal Shiva linga worn on the body, symbol of formless God)
Kayaka (work is worship, dignity of all labor)
Vachana (devotional prose-poems in Kannada as scripture and spiritual practice)
Anubhava Mantapa (experiential assembly where people of all castes shared spiritual insights)
Ashtavarana (eight spiritual aids/protections for devotees)
Rejection of caste system, Vedic authority, and temple idol worship
Gender equality in spiritual practice
Dasoha (sharing of one's labor and wealth with the community)
traditions.evidence: Thousands of extant vachana poems in medieval Kannada, Basava Purana (13th c. hagiography), Sunya Sampadane (compilation of sharana dialogues), living Lingayat community of approximately 20 million in Karnataka. UNESCO recognized Basavanna's contributions to democratic thought.

=== CONNECTIONS ===: Array of connections between new and existing traditions

connections.evidence: Scythians were Iranian-speaking Indo-Europeans; their deities (Tabiti, Papaeus) derive from Proto-Indo-Iranian divine concepts. Linguistic analysis confirms Iranian etymologies for Scythian divine names.
Sky-father worship and tripartite cosmos in Tengrism show possible Indo-European substrate influence. Tengri concept may share deep roots with PIE *Dyeus, though Turkic-Mongolic origins are primary.
Both traditions share Indo-Iranian roots. Papaeus parallels Ahura Mazda, Tabiti parallels fire worship (Atar). Herodotus noted similarities. Scythians interacted with Persians extensively.
Tengrism developed from prehistoric Siberian shamanic substrate. World-tree cosmology, shamanic ecstasy, spirit-helper concepts, and sky-worship all derive from broader Siberian shamanic traditions.
Mongolian shamanism shares world-tree cosmology, drum-journeying, and spirit-helper traditions with broader Siberian shamanism. Buryat shamanism bridges the two traditions geographically.
Mongolian shamanism (Boo Morgol) incorporated Tengri as chief deity with 99 Tengers. Secret History of the Mongols records Tengri worship as central. The two traditions are deeply intertwined.

Bon and Tibetan Buddhism heavily influenced each other from 7th century CE onward. Dzogchen tradition is shared. Bon adopted Buddhist monastic structures; Buddhism absorbed Bon protective deities and rituals.

Tibetan Buddhism was adopted by Mongol khans from 13th century; by 16th century under Altan Khan, Buddhism became dominant while absorbing shamanic elements. Many shamanic spirits were reinterpreted as Buddhist protectors.

Korean shamanism shares Siberian shamanic features: ecstatic trance, spirit possession, world-tree cosmology, drum usage, bear mythology. Dangun bear-woman myth parallels Siberian bear cults. Migration routes via Manchuria.

Muism shares core Siberian shamanic elements: ecstatic spirit possession, bear mythology (Dangun myth), mountain god veneration, cosmological three-world structure. Korean peninsula was a southern terminus of Siberian shamanic diffusion.

Theravada Buddhism integrated with Tai animism from 13th century Sukhothai period onward. Buddhist temples coexist with spirit houses. Monks perform merit-making rituals alongside phi spirit ceremonies.
Hindu Brahmanical elements entered Tai religion through Khmer and Mon intermediaries. Royal court rituals, Brahmin priests at Thai court, spirit house design, and deity concepts show Hindu influence.

Indian Hindu traders and priests brought Shaiva and Vaishnava traditions to Bali from 1st century CE. Prasasti inscriptions from 9th century confirm established Hindu kingdoms. Majapahit refugees (1478) reinforced Hindu culture.

Balinese Hinduism absorbed Buddhist elements through centuries of Hindu-Buddhist coexistence in Javanese kingdoms (Majapahit, Sailendra). Buddhist Padmasana shrines found alongside Hindu temples throughout Bali.
Over a millennium of Chinese rule introduced Taoist concepts to Vietnamese folk religion. Mother Goddess hierarchy, palace cosmology, and spirit medium practices show Taoist structural influence.
Confucian ethics of filial piety reinforced existing Vietnamese ancestor worship. Confucian scholarly culture shaped the literary and ritual dimensions of Dao Mau practice.

Buddhist concepts of compassion and karma integrated with Vietnamese folk beliefs. Dao Mau temples often include Buddhist altars. Many practitioners simultaneously follow Buddhism and Mother Goddess worship.

Caodaism emerged from Vietnamese spiritual culture, incorporating spiritism from Dao Mau medium traditions. Founder Ngo Van Chieu drew on Vietnamese folk religion's spirit communication practices alongside Buddhist, Taoist, and Christian elements.
Caodaism explicitly incorporates Buddhist karma, rebirth, and vegetarian ethics. Sakyamuni Buddha is honored as a spiritual figure within the Cao Dai pantheon.

Caodaism adopts Taoist cosmology, spiritual cultivation practices, and deified figures including Li Bai (Ly Thai Bach). Taoist occult practices informed the seance methods central to Caodai revelation.
Caodaism incorporates Confucian ethical precepts, filial piety, and social hierarchy. Confucius is honored as a spiritual figure in the Cao Dai pantheon.

Caodaism borrowed Catholic hierarchical organization (Pope, Cardinals, Bishops), the All-Seeing Eye symbol, and concept of divine revelation. French colonial Catholicism was a major influence on the founders.

Hindu-Buddhist kingdoms (Mataram, Majapahit) provided the theological layer of Kejawen. Sanskrit terminology, deity concepts (Dewi Sri, Batara Guru/Shiva), and meditation practices entered Javanese mysticism through Indian influence.

Buddhist meditation techniques and concepts of enlightenment entered Javanese culture through Sailendra dynasty (8th-9th century). Borobudur temple reflects Buddhist-Javanese synthesis that influenced Kejawen.

Sufi Islam arrived via traders in 15th century and blended with Javanese mysticism through the Wali Songo (Nine Saints). Sufi emphasis on inner spiritual journey (batin) matched Javanese kebatinan perfectly.

Hindu-Buddhist cultural influence reached Philippines through Sri Vijaya and Majapahit trade networks. Bathala may derive from Sanskrit Bhattara (noble lord). Agusan gold image shows Hindu deity style. Philippine scripts derived from Indic scripts.

Ayyavazhi emerged from Hindu theological matrix, using Vaishnavite avatar concept (Vaikundar as incarnation of Narayana). The Akilam scripture references Hindu puranic cosmology extensively while reinterpreting it.

Lingayatism arose as a radical reform of Hindu Shaivism, retaining devotion to Shiva while rejecting Vedic authority, caste system, and temple worship. Basavanna's vachanas explicitly critique orthodox Hindu practices.

connections.note: Muism and the existing korean_shamanism entry represent the same tradition at different depths. Muism is the expanded scholarly treatment with deeper coverage of Dangun mythology, Sanshin, and mudang practices.

connections.evidence: Buddhism arrived in Korea in 372 CE and absorbed many shamanic deities as dharma protectors. Sanshin shrines appear in Buddhist temple compounds. Buddhist Seonangdang and shamanic practices coexist at many sacred sites.
Confucian ancestor veneration reinforced existing Korean ancestral practices in Muism. However, Joseon-era Neo-Confucian orthodoxy actively suppressed mudang shamanism as superstition.

Scythian and Zoroastrian traditions share Indo-Iranian fire worship (Tabiti/Atar), sky-god concepts, and ritual practices. Achaemenid-Scythian cultural exchange documented by Herodotus. Some scholars view Scythian religion as a parallel Iranian development.

=== FIGURES ===: Array of shared/notable figures across traditions

figures.name: Tengri
figures.dateLabel: Prehistoric (attested from Xiongnu period, c. 3rd century BCE)

roles.tengrism: Supreme sky god, Eternal Blue Sky, ruler of all existence and bestower of divine mandate (kut) to khans
roles.mongolian_shamanism: Chief of the 99 Tengers (Qormusta Tengri), supreme heavenly father
roles.siberian_shamanism: Sky deity (Tangara among Yakuts), ruler of the Upper World

connections.detail: Both are supreme sky-father deities. Some scholars propose deep etymological connections between Tengri and PIE *Dyeus, though this is debated.

roles.tengrism: Chosen of Tengri, united the Mongol peoples under Tengrism as state religion, instituted religious tolerance
roles.mongolian_shamanism: Deified ancestor spirit (sulde), supreme national figure whose spirit protects Mongolia

connections.detail: Genghis Khan attributed all his conquests to the grace and mandate of Tengri, as recorded in the Secret History of the Mongols

roles.bon: Supreme Teacher and enlightened founder of Bon, equivalent of a Buddha, born in Olmo Lungring to teach liberation from samsara

connections.detail: Tonpa Shenrab is the Bon equivalent of Shakyamuni Buddha; Bon tradition claims he attained enlightenment several hundred years before Siddhartha Gautama

roles.scythian_religion: Supreme goddess of fire and the hearth, most venerated Scythian deity, the 'Burning One' from whom all creation emerged

connections.detail: Tabiti's role as sacred fire deity parallels the Zoroastrian fire concept Atar; both derive from Indo-Iranian fire worship traditions

roles.scythian_religion: Sky father god, personification of Heaven, consort of Api (Earth), equated with Zeus by Herodotus

connections.detail: Papaeus parallels Ahura Mazda as supreme Indo-Iranian sky-father deity. Both derive from Proto-Indo-Iranian divine father concept.

roles.muism: Legendary divine founder of Gojoseon, first Korean nation; son of heavenly prince Hwanung and bear-woman Ungnyeo; later became Sanshin (Mountain God)
roles.korean_shamanism: Founding ancestor of the Korean people, divine origin myth connecting heaven and earth

connections.detail: According to Korean mythology, Dangun became the Sanshin (Mountain God) after ruling for over 1,000 years, connecting political founding to shamanic mountain worship

figures.name: Sanshin (Mountain God)
figures.dateLabel: Mythological (associated with Dangun, 2333 BCE traditional date)

roles.muism: Mountain God, protector of Korean land, depicted as white-bearded elder with tiger companion; each Korean mountain hosts a unique Sanshin
roles.korean_shamanism: Guardian spirit of mountains, central deity of Korean shamanic landscape, worshipped at shrines (sansindang) throughout Korean mountains

connections.detail: Dangun became the archetypal Sanshin after abdicating the throne, making mountain god worship inseparable from Korean national origin mythology

figures.name: Bathala
figures.dateLabel: Pre-colonial (ancient Tagalog belief, predates Spanish contact 1521)

roles.philippine_indigenous_religion: Supreme creator god (Maykapal/Lumikha) of the Tagalog people, all-powerful creator of the universe and humanity

connections.detail: The name Bathala may derive from Sanskrit Bhattara (noble lord), reflecting Hindu-Buddhist cultural influence on pre-colonial Philippine religion through maritime trade networks

figures.name: Dewi Sri
figures.dateLabel: Ancient Javanese (pre-Hindu period, possibly Bronze Age origins)

roles.kejawen: Rice goddess of fertility and prosperity, central to Javanese agricultural ritual and slametan ceremonies
roles.balinese_hinduism: Goddess of rice and fertility, honored with elaborate offerings during planting and harvest cycles

connections.detail: Dewi Sri is often identified with the Hindu goddess Sri Lakshmi, reflecting the Hindu-Javanese syncretism where an indigenous rice spirit was merged with the Indian goddess of abundance

figures.name: Lieu Hanh (Mau Lieu Hanh)

roles.vietnamese_folk_religion: Mother Goddess of the Earthly Realm, one of Vietnam's Four Immortals (Tu Bat Tu), chief deity of Dao Mau, celestial princess incarnated on earth

aliases: Ba Chua Lieu (Lady of the Willow)

roles.caodaism: Founding prophet and first medium to receive divine revelations from Cao Dai (Supreme Being) during seances in the 1920s; French colonial official turned religious leader

roles.ayyavazhi: Purna avatar (full incarnation) of Narayana, sent to destroy the evil Kaliyan/Kroni and inaugurate the age of righteousness (Dharma Yukam); social reformer who preached caste equality

connections.detail: Ayyavazhi theology identifies Vaikundar as the final and complete avatar of Narayana/Vishnu, superseding the traditional Dashavatara list

roles.lingayat: Founder of Lingayatism, philosopher-poet-statesman who established the Anubhava Mantapa and promoted radical social equality through the vachana literary movement
roles.hinduism: 12th-century Shaivite bhakti saint and social reformer from Karnataka; prime minister under King Bijjala II of Kalachuri dynasty

connections.detail: Basavanna's entire philosophy centers on direct, personal devotion to Shiva through the ishtalinga, without priestly intermediaries or temple rituals

roles.lingayat: Prominent female mystic poet and radical renunciant; composed vachanas addressed to 'Lord White as Jasmine' (Chennamallikarjuna); icon of spiritual gender equality

connections.detail: Akka Mahadevi was a contemporary and fellow member of Basavanna's Anubhava Mantapa spiritual assembly; one of the most prominent female voices in the vachana movement

figures.name: Umay
figures.dateLabel: Prehistoric Turkic (attested in Orkhon inscriptions, 8th century CE)

roles.tengrism: Mother goddess of fertility, virginity, children, and the hearth; protectress of women and newborns; consort or feminine complement of Tengri

connections.detail: Both Umay and Tabiti serve as hearth/fire-related mother goddesses in Inner Asian traditions, reflecting Indo-Iranian and Turkic parallel developments of hearth deity concepts

roles.tengrism: God of death and the underworld (Lower World), first human who was cast down after rebelling against Tengri
roles.siberian_shamanism: Lord of the dead and ruler of the Lower World among Altai and Turkic Siberian peoples
roles.mongolian_shamanism: Underworld lord opposed to the 99 Tengers, ruler of dark spirits

connections.detail: Erlik parallels the Vedic/Buddhist death god Yama. Some scholars trace both to a common Indo-European or Inner Asian death-deity archetype connected to the first mortal who became lord of the dead.

figures.name: Ratu Kidul (Nyai Roro Kidul)
figures.dateLabel: Ancient Javanese (pre-Hindu period; formalized in Mataram era, 16th-17th century)

roles.kejawen: Queen of the South Sea (Indian Ocean), powerful spirit consort of Javanese kings; commander of the spirit armies; patron of Javanese royalty from Mataram dynasty onward

roles.muism: Abandoned seventh princess who journeyed to the underworld to obtain the water of life for her parents; patron saint of mudang shamans and guide of souls to the afterlife

connections.detail: Both Dangun and Princess Bari serve as foundational mythological figures of Muism: Dangun connects heaven and earth through descent, while Bari connects the living and dead through her underworld journey
